title: What Marketing Is: Concepts & Orientations
simple_html:
Marketing is often confused with advertising or selling, but it is much broader. At its heart, marketing is the process of creating, communicating, and delivering value to customers in a way that also benefits the organization. The American Marketing Association defines it as the set of activities and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers and society.

Why it matters

A business only survives if it gives people something they are willing to pay for. Marketing is the discipline that figures out what people need, designs an offering to meet that need, and makes sure the right people know about it and can buy it. Done well, marketing turns a one-time sale into a long-term, profitable relationship.

Core building blocks

  • Needs are basic requirements (food, safety, belonging). Wants are needs shaped by culture and personality (a specific cuisine). Demands are wants backed by buying power.
  • Value is the benefit a customer gets minus the cost (money, time, effort) they pay. Customers choose whatever they believe offers the most value.
  • Exchange is the act of trading something of value for the offering; marketing makes exchanges happen smoothly.

Business orientations

Companies focus their efforts differently. A production orientation chases efficiency and low cost; a product orientation prizes quality and features; a selling orientation pushes hard to move what it makes; and a marketing orientation starts with the customer and works backward. The most advanced firms adopt a holistic/societal orientation that also weighs employees, partners, and society.

Key takeaways

  • Marketing creates and delivers value, not just promotion.
  • Needs, wants, demands, value, and exchange are the foundation concepts.
  • Customer-centric (marketing) orientation outperforms inside-out (selling) thinking.
  • Great marketing builds lasting, profitable customer relationships.
intermediate_html:
Marketing is the organizational function and set of processes for identifying customer needs and satisfying them profitably. It spans far more than promotion: it includes understanding markets, designing offerings, setting prices, building distribution, and communicating value. This lesson establishes the vocabulary and the strategic mindset that the rest of the course builds on.

The core concepts chain

Marketing thinking flows through a logical chain. Needs (states of felt deprivation) become wants (needs directed at specific objects by culture and personality), which become demands when backed by purchasing power. Marketers then craft offerings (a blend of products, services, and experiences) that deliver value — the ratio of perceived benefits to total cost. When a customer trades money for the offering, an exchange occurs, and a series of exchanges builds a relationship. The marketer's job is to maximize customer-perceived value relative to competitors.

The five marketing orientations (philosophies)

OrientationCore beliefRisk
ProductionCustomers want cheap, available products; focus on efficiencyMarketing myopia — ignores real needs
ProductCustomers prefer the best quality/featuresBuilds a "better mousetrap" no one wants
SellingCustomers must be persuaded to buy what we makeAggressive push, weak loyalty
MarketingStart with target customers' needs, then deliver value better than rivalsCan miss long-run societal effects
Holistic/SocietalIntegrate all stakeholders and society's long-run welfareComplex to coordinate

The marketing mix (4Ps)

Once a firm knows its target, it deploys the controllable 4Ps: Product (the offering and its features), Price (what is charged and how), Place (distribution and availability), and Promotion (communication). For services, three more Ps are added — People, Process, and Physical evidence (the 7Ps). The 4Ps are the seller's view; the customer's view is sometimes reframed as the 4Cs (Customer value, Cost, Convenience, Communication).

Worked mini-case

Consider a startup selling premium reusable water bottles. A selling orientation would hire pushy salespeople to clear inventory. A marketing orientation instead researches that target customers want bottles that keep drinks cold for 24 hours and signal eco-conscious identity (wants), and that gym-goers with disposable income will pay a premium (demands). The firm designs an insulated, stylish bottle (Product), prices it as a premium item (Price), sells through fitness studios and online (Place), and tells a sustainability story (Promotion). Value = (cold drinks + style + green identity) minus price and effort to buy.

How to apply it

  • Diagnose any firm's orientation by asking: does it start with the customer or with the factory?
  • Map an offering against the 4Ps to check for consistency (a premium product sold cheaply through discounters sends mixed signals).
  • Always express the offering as value delivered, not features produced.

This foundation connects forward to the marketing environment (Lesson 2), where you analyze the forces that shape needs and demand, and to STP (Lesson 4), where you choose which customers to serve.
full_html:
The opening lesson of marketing management defines the field, distinguishes it from related activities such as selling and advertising, and equips the learner with the conceptual scaffolding — needs, wants, demands, value, exchange, offerings, markets — and the strategic philosophies that govern how firms approach customers. Mastery here is foundational: every later topic (segmentation, branding, pricing, channels, communications, and metrics) presupposes a clear grasp of what marketing fundamentally is.

Defining marketing

The American Marketing Association (AMA, 2017) defines marketing as "the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large." Kotler and Keller (2016) offer a managerial complement: marketing management is "the art and science of choosing target markets and getting, keeping, and growing customers through creating, delivering, and communicating superior customer value." A famous social definition holds that the aim of marketing is "to make selling superfluous" (Drucker, 1973) — if a firm understands its customer so well that the product fits and sells itself, the hard-sell becomes unnecessary. This distinction between marketing (creating value customers want) and selling (persuading customers to take what exists) recurs throughout the discipline.

The core concepts

Marketing rests on an interlocking set of primitives. Needs are basic human requirements — physical (food, shelter, safety), social (belonging, esteem), and individual (knowledge, self-expression) — and are not created by marketers. Wants are needs shaped by culture and individual personality; the need for nourishment becomes a want for a particular cuisine. Demands are wants for specific products backed by ability and willingness to pay. Critics who accuse marketing of "creating needs" conflate these levels: marketers influence wants and stimulate demand, but do not manufacture the underlying need (Levitt, 1960).

Value is the central organizing idea. Customer-perceived value is the difference between the prospective customer's evaluation of all the benefits and all the costs of an offering relative to alternatives. Benefits include functional, emotional, and self-expressive dimensions; costs include monetary, time, energy, and psychological costs. Customer satisfaction is the post-purchase comparison of perceived performance against expectations, and it drives customer loyalty and lifetime value (linked forward to Lesson 10). An exchange — the act of obtaining a desired object by offering something in return — is marketing's core mechanism, and a market is the set of actual and potential buyers of an offering.

Marketing orientations: the evolution of philosophy

Firms have historically organized around five competing philosophies (Kotler & Keller, 2016). The production concept holds that consumers favor widely available, inexpensive products and that the firm should concentrate on production efficiency and broad distribution — appropriate in supply-constrained or price-sensitive markets but dangerous when it ignores latent needs. The product concept assumes consumers prefer products offering the most quality, performance, or innovative features; its peril is the "better-mousetrap fallacy," in which engineers perfect a product customers do not want. The selling concept assumes consumers, left alone, will not buy enough, so the firm must mount aggressive selling and promotion; it is common with unsought goods (insurance) and overcapacity, but it pursues transactions over relationships and risks dissatisfied buyers.

The marketing concept, emerging in the 1950s, inverts the logic: rather than "make and sell," the firm "senses and responds," starting with a well-defined target market, focusing on customer needs, coordinating all customer-affecting activities, and achieving profitability through customer satisfaction. Theodore Levitt's seminal "Marketing Myopia" (1960) crystallized the danger of product-centric thinking: railroads declined because they defined themselves as in the railroad business rather than the transportation business, ignoring customer needs served by cars and planes. Finally, the holistic (societal) marketing concept recognizes the breadth and interdependence of marketing, integrating four components — relationship marketing (building mutually satisfying long-term ties with customers, employees, channel partners, and the financial community), integrated marketing (coordinating all marketing activities for consistent value), internal marketing (ensuring everyone in the organization embraces marketing principles), and performance marketing (accounting for financial and non-financial returns, including ethics, environment, legal, and social context).

The marketing mix: 4Ps and beyond

McCarthy (1960) codified the firm's tactical toolkit as the 4Ps: Product, Price, Place (distribution), and Promotion. These are the controllable variables a firm blends to produce the response it wants in the target market. Booms and Bitner (1981) extended the framework to services with three additional Ps — People, Process, and Physical evidence — yielding the 7Ps, in recognition that services are intangible, inseparable, variable, and perishable (developed in services-marketing lessons). Lauterborn (1990) reframed the seller-centric 4Ps into the customer-centric 4Cs: Customer value, Cost to the customer, Convenience, and Communication. The reframing is pedagogically powerful because it forces an outside-in perspective consistent with the marketing concept.

Worked example

Suppose two coffee firms compete. Firm A (selling orientation) over-roasts to cut cost, prices low, and runs constant discount promotions to clear stock; churn is high and brand perception weak. Firm B (marketing/holistic orientation) researches that target customers value ethically sourced beans, a third-place ambience, and convenience. It designs the offering (Product), prices at a premium justified by perceived value (Price), locates near commuter flows and offers a mobile app (Place), and communicates an authentic sustainability narrative (Promotion), while training baristas (People), streamlining ordering (Process), and curating store design (Physical evidence). Customer-perceived value — benefits (taste, identity, experience, convenience) minus costs (price, time) — is higher for B, producing satisfaction, loyalty, and superior lifetime value despite higher prices. This illustrates why a marketing orientation typically outperforms a selling orientation over the long run.

Common misconceptions and debates

  • "Marketing equals advertising." Promotion is one of four Ps; marketing also encompasses product design, pricing, and distribution decisions made long before any ad runs.
  • "Marketing creates needs." Marketing shapes wants and channels demand but works upon pre-existing human needs.
  • The marketing-concept critique. Scholars note that slavishly following expressed customer wants can stifle breakthrough innovation; customers could not have asked for the smartphone. A customer-led firm risks incrementalism, whereas a market-driving firm shapes latent demand (Jaworski, Kohli & Sahay, 2000). Mastery means holding both: listen to customers and lead them.
  • Societal tension. The societal marketing concept asks firms to balance company profit, customer wants, and society's long-run welfare — a tension visible in debates over sustainability and ethical marketing (developed in later ethics lessons).

Cross-links and synthesis

This lesson sets up the entire syllabus. Understanding the environment that shapes needs and demand follows in The Marketing Environment & Research (Lesson 2); choosing which customers to serve and how to be perceived follows in Segmentation, Targeting & Positioning (Lesson 4); the value-delivery mechanics unfold across product, pricing, channel, and communications lessons; and the relationship/performance dimensions of holistic marketing culminate in Customer Value, CLV & Marketing Metrics (Lesson 10). The throughline is constant: marketing exists to create and capture superior customer value profitably.
